name: Shark God
description: In Hawaiian mythology, shark gods, or *mano kaluha*, are powerful and respected figures, often considered 'aumakua, or family guardians. They can appear as sharks, humans, or a combination of both. These deities embody both the benevolent and malevolent aspects of the ocean, capable of protecting their followers from danger and ensuring abundant fishing, but also of inflicting punishment on those who disrespect the sea or break kapu (sacred laws). Some prominent shark gods include Kamohoali'i, the elder brother of Pele, and Kauhuhu, a powerful shark god who could transform into a human. Offerings and prayers were often given to shark gods to appease them and ensure safe passage on the ocean. Their presence underscores the deep connection between the Hawaiian people and the ocean, highlighting the vital role sharks played in their ecosystem and spiritual beliefs. Stories of shark gods serve as reminders of the ocean's power and the importance of respecting its boundaries.
subtitle: Guardians of the Deep, Lords of the Sea
appearance: Shark gods can manifest in various forms. Often, they appear as massive sharks, larger and more powerful than any ordinary shark, sometimes with glowing eyes or unique markings. They can also take human form, often with shark teeth, piercing eyes, or other subtle shark-like features. Some tales describe them as humanoids with shark fins or tails, bridging the gap between the human and marine worlds. Their presence is often accompanied by a change in the ocean's currents or the appearance of other marine life.
habitat: Shark gods primarily inhabit the ocean, ranging from shallow coastal waters to the deepest trenches. They are often associated with specific reefs, bays, or islands considered sacred. Some are believed to reside in underwater caves or hidden realms within the ocean. They can also venture onto land in human form, visiting villages or interacting with mortals.
behavior: The behavior of shark gods varies depending on their individual personalities and roles. Some are benevolent protectors, guiding lost sailors, providing abundant fishing, and healing the sick. Others are fierce warriors, punishing those who disrespect the ocean or break sacred laws. Many are known for their wisdom and ability to foresee the future. They are generally respected and feared, demanding reverence and adherence to traditional customs.

keyMyths.title: Kamohoali'i and Pele
keyMyths.description: Kamohoali'i, the shark god and elder brother of the volcano goddess Pele, often protected her during her travels and aided her in her endeavors. He is a prominent figure in many Hawaiian legends, showcasing the complex relationships between deities.

keyMyths.title: Kauhuhu and the Human Form
keyMyths.description: Kauhuhu, a powerful shark god, could transform into a human and often interacted with humans, sometimes marrying into families and bestowing blessings or curses based on their actions. This highlights the interconnectedness of the human and divine realms.

sources.author: Martha Warren Beckwith
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Hawaiian myths and legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Hawaiian myths and legends.

sources.author: Herb Kawainui Kane
sources.description: A study of ancient Hawaiian culture and beliefs, including the role of shark gods.
